headlineSummary: Anthropic drew the most negative reaction on trust, pricing, and access concerns, while OpenAI and Google Gemini were more mixed with limited evidence for Gemini [E2][E4][E6][E1].

ranking.summary: Anthropic was viewed negatively overall, with repeated complaints about closed access, pricing, and damaged trust, though one comment remained supportive of Claude Code [E2][E3][E4][E5][E6][E7]
Google Gemini had limited evidence and a mostly neutral read, with one criticism focused on deprecating the open source Gemini CLI in favor of a closed-source replacement [E2]
OpenAI landed in mixed-neutral territory, with criticism of hype and pricing offset by one comment praising Codex for helping non-technical users build independently [E1][E3][E7]

evidence.excerpt: This weird trend reached an apex in a Feb 2026 OpenAI blog post [1], recently on the front page [2], which describes the process for building... something... written 100% by agents. There is no description of what the thing is, no indication of what value it provides its users. The closest it gets is "the product has been used by hundreds of users internally, including daily internal power users". But the fact that the thing has a million lines of code is repeated twice in the first few hundred words.

annotations.rationale: The commenter criticizes an OpenAI blog post as content-light and overly focused on hype about code volume.

evidence.excerpt: The industry has been moving the wrong direction with Claude Code staying closed (despite multiple times leaking the source code!) and the open source Gemini CLI being deprecated in favor of closed source Antigravity CLI.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Claude Code staying closed is moving the industry in the wrong direction.
The commenter criticizes deprecating the open source Gemini CLI in favor of a closed source replacement.

evidence.excerpt: Claude and Codex pricing will eventually have to come down, for most common coding tasks you don't need a super smart slow model but a smart-enough and very fast one.

annotations.rationale: The comment implies Claude is priced too high for common coding tasks.
The comment implies Codex is priced too high for common coding tasks.

evidence.excerpt: This has dampened my opinion on Anthropic quite a bit. It's difficult to take their marketing for AI as an empowering technology seriously when they are quite clear in their new deployments that they do not mean empowering for you, but empowering for them and organizations that are in their (or the US government's, despite Anthropics performative disagreements with the administration) good graces.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says their opinion of Anthropic has worsened substantially and accuses the company of self-serving, insincere empowerment claims.

evidence.excerpt: I like Claude Code a lot, I think it sets a dangerous precedent to put guardrails in that return a response from a prompt that was modified by the system in real time in order to subvert the original intent. Fail cleanly. Anything else makes it too difficult to rely on.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly likes Claude Code but criticizes invisible guardrails as dangerous and unreliable, making the sentiment mixed overall.

evidence.excerpt: The reputational damage has been done. This is the sort of thing that cannot be unsaid -- the presumption is they will just do it in secret now. Anthropic's "we're the good guys" PR campaign is dead.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says Anthropic's trust and reputation are badly damaged and suggests future secrecy is presumed.

evidence.excerpt: Once done, non-technical people can continue developing independently without hiring any software engineers by simply telling claude/codex what they want. Claude/codex will then be able to make judgement calls based on the preset architecture, which will guide the non-technical user.

annotations.rationale: The commenter claims Claude can enable non-technical users to keep building independently and make judgment calls within an architecture.
The commenter claims Codex can enable non-technical users to keep building independently and make judgment calls within an architecture.
